"We are not obedient because we are blind. We are obedient because we can see."

Elder Boyd K. Packer

New Testament

Lesson 30: "God Is No Respecter of Persons"

Class Handout

Six feet six inches, tattooed, flannel shirt and cut-offs, riding a Harley

Peggy and I went to visit our two sons in Mesa, AZ during the first week of June. That Sunday, during fast and testimony meeting, a young missionary got up and bore his testimony. He told of “a gentleman riding on his Harley, six feet six inches tall, tattooed, wearing a flannel shirt and cut-offs. His business is failing. He says a little prayer and hears, ‘You need to go to church.’ So he stopped at the first church and it happened to be an LDS church. He parked his Harley and walked in. At the door he was greeted by a man who invited him to sit with his family.” Then he said, “This is the story of my stake president.”

After the meeting I went to talk with that young missionary. His name was Elder Boleski and his companion was Elder Clark. Elder Boleski said he was born in Canada, but his family moved a lot. Eventually his family moved to Norman, Oklahoma where he left on his mission. The gentleman on the Harley was Robert Hays, his stake president in Norman, Oklahoma. Elder Boleski gave me verbal permission to retell President Hays’ story.

An Angel visits the Roman Centurion, Cornelius

Cornelius had also been praying and fasting for four days, when the Lord sent an angel to visit him with a message (Acts 10:30-33) and Cornelius, just like Robert Hays, obeyed. Peter himself had received a revelation in a dream given to him three times, before Cornelius’s messengers reached him (Acts 10:9-20). In the dream, the Lord emphasized: “What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common” (Acts 10:15). Furthermore, as Peter was pondering the meaning of the dream, the Spirit told him: “Behold, three men seek thee. Arise therefore and get thee down, and go with them, doubting nothing: for I have sent them” (Acts 10:19-20). We know now, from history, that this series of revelations changed the world.

Peter is imprisoned, but the saints pray for him and he is miraculously freed

King Herod Agrippa I, a grandson of Herod the Great, was an observer of the law of Moses and he sought to retain the favor of the Jews (Acts 12:1-5). (See The Life and Teachings of Jesus and His Apostles (30-9) Acts 12:1. Who were Herod and Bernice?) Herod Agrippa I set a guard of four squads of four men each (“four quaternions of soldiers”), to ensure Peter could not escape. However, Peter was sustained by the unceasing prayers of the members of the church and God sent an angel to free him.

This miracle along with so many other miracles, make it seem clear that so long as the saints are righteous and so long as the church has apostles and prophets to lead and guide the church, that it cannot be defeated. The Prophet Joseph Smith said: “Our missionaries are going forth to different nations, and in Germany, Palestine, New Holland, Australia, the East Indies, and other places, the Standard of Truth has been erected; no unhallowed hand can stop the work from progressing; persecutions may rage, mobs may combine, armies may assemble, calumny may defame, but the truth of God will go forth boldly, nobly, and independent, till it has penetrated every continent, visited every clime, swept every country, and sounded in every ear, till the purposes of God shall be accomplished, and the Great Jehovah shall say the work is done” (History of the Church, 4:540. See “Latter-day Prophets Speak about Missionary Service,” Liahona, Oct. 2001).

The Apostles determine that Gentiles do not need to keep the law of Moses

As we read Acts 15:6-31, we can see a pattern by which, Church policy and practices are made. First, Church leaders meet to consider the matter (verse 6). Second, they discuss the matter thoroughly (verses 7-21). Third, they make a decision in accordance with the Lord’s will (verses 19-21). Fourth, the Holy Ghost confirms that the decision is correct (verse 28). Fifth, the decision is announced to the Saints for sustaining (verses 22-31).

Compare this procedure with the description of President Spencer W. Kimball in “Thoughts and Events Leading up to the Declaration”  (After clicking on the link, scroll down to the “Thoughts and Events” section on this web page.) President Kimball describes going to the upper rooms of the temple to pour out his soul in prayer, wanting to do the Lord’s will. He had asked each member of the Quorum of the Twelve “to carefully research the scriptures and statements of the earlier brethren, to make an exhaustive study of all that had been recorded concerning this issue. For months before the revelation, the First Presidency and the Quorum of the Twelve discussed these sacred mattes at length in their temple meetings. He also met privately with each of the brethren to learn their feelings on the matter.”

Then, at a Fast and Testimony meeting in the Temple, they discussed the priesthood for two more hours in the temple before praying around the altar. Elder Bruce R. McConkie testified: “It was during this prayer that the answer came.” Elder David B. Haight said, “We witnessed an outpouring of the spirit which bonded our souls together in perfect unity—a glorious experience.” On another occasion, Elder Haight said of the experience, “No one could say anything because of the powerful outpouring of the heavenly spiritual experience.” 

I remember where I was when I learned of the Revelation concerning the Priesthood. I was in Ogden, Utah near the intersection of 24th and Wall. I will never forget my excitement. I am certain the Lord continues to lead and guide His Church as He did during Peter’s time.

Questions for pondering and discussion:

Here are six questions to think about and discuss: 1) How did some Church members respond, on hearing that Peter taught the gospel to Gentiles? 2) How should we receive new instructions form our Church leaders? 3) How can our prayers help the prophet and Church leaders today? 4) Why are prayer, fasting, revelation, and authority important in calling members to serve? 5) What experiences have helped you know that Church callings are inspired by the Spirit? 6) How do the events of Acts 15:6-31 describe the pattern by which, Church policy and practices are made?
